skill.name: pyhealth
skill.description: Guidance for using PyHealth to build healthcare machine learning workflows with clinical datasets, prediction tasks, medical codes, models, preprocessing, and evaluation.
skill.summary: Use PyHealth references to plan clinical machine learning datasets, tasks, models, preprocessing, and evaluation.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but reviewed examples show Markdown code spans, fenced Python examples, clinical coding terms, and documentation links. No prompt injection, malicious instructions, credential access, destructive commands, or runtime network behavior were found in the reviewed skill files. The remaining network evidence is limited to two public documentation links in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown code spans flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown inline code and fenced Python examples, such as PyHealth imports and method names. They do not execute shell commands or request command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are documentation syntax and Python examples. No shell interpreter, subprocess call, or user-controlled command path is present.

low_findings.title: False positive: Clinical terminology flagged as weak cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur in healthcare model and medical coding documentation, including ICD, NDC, ATC, model names, and numeric clinical codes. No cryptographic API or hashing implementation was found in these contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines describe clinical codes, model counts, and preprocessing parameters. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False positive: Python imports flagged as dynamic import expressions
low_findings.description: The dynamic import detections are standard Python from-import statements inside fenced examples. They are not JavaScript dynamic import calls and do not load untrusted modules at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a static Python example in Markdown. The syntax is not a dynamic import expression and is not executable by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: False positive: Reconnaissance detections in clinical documentation
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections map to benign healthcare and model documentation, including drug safety discussion, model device settings, and attention analysis examples. No system probing or network scanning instructions were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines discuss clinical analysis or tensor indexing. There are no commands like host discovery, port scanning, or operating system enumeration.

low_findings.title: Low-risk documentation links only
low_findings.description: Two hardcoded URLs point to public PyHealth documentation and GitHub issues. They are reference links in Markdown, not hidden network calls or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible user-facing resources for documentation and support. No code in the skill sends data to these URLs.

content.user_title: Build Healthcare AI Models with PyHealth
content.value_statement: Healthcare machine learning requires careful dataset, task, model, and evaluation choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PyHealth workflows using focused references.
actual_capabilities: Explains PyHealth dataset classes for MIMIC, eICU, OMOP, EEG, imaging, and text datasets.
Guides selection of clinical prediction tasks such as mortality, readmission, length of stay, and drug recommendation.
Summarizes medical code mapping with ICD, CCS, NDC, RxNorm, and ATC systems.
Compares PyHealth model families, including baselines, CNN, RNN, Transformer, RETAIN, SafeDrug, and graph models.
Describes preprocessing, data splitting, training, evaluation, calibration, fairness, and interpretability practices.
limitations: Does not include the PyHealth library source code or execute models by itself.
Requires users to have lawful access to clinical datasets before loading them.
Does not replace clinical review, regulatory validation, or patient safety governance.
Examples may need adaptation for local schemas, hardware, privacy rules, and PyHealth version differences.

use_cases.title: Plan an EHR prediction pipeline
use_cases.description: Select a dataset, task function, split strategy, model, and metrics for mortality or readmission prediction.

use_cases.title: Standardize medical code features
use_cases.description: Map diagnosis, procedure, and medication codes across ICD, CCS, NDC, RxNorm, and ATC systems.

use_cases.title: Review model readiness for research
use_cases.description: Check preprocessing, calibration, fairness, interpretability, and evaluation choices before reporting results.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a PyHealth dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a clinical prediction project with PyHealth. Compare the available dataset options for my data type, task, and access constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Design a prediction task
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define a PyHealth prediction task for [mortality/readmission/length of stay/drug recommendation]. Include inputs, labels, split strategy, and leakage checks.

prompt_templates.title: Select and train a model
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend PyHealth models for my clinical dataset and task. Explain feature keys, mode, training settings, metrics, and baseline comparisons.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a clinical ML workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PyHealth workflow for clinical machine learning risks. Focus on data leakage, calibration, fairness, interpretability, and deployment limitations.

output_examples.input: User asks which PyHealth dataset and task to use for ICU mortality prediction.
output_examples.output: The response recommends MIMIC-IV or eICU, a mortality prediction task, patient-level splitting, baseline models, Transformer or RETAIN, and PR-AUC evaluation.

output_examples.input: User asks how to normalize medication codes before drug recommendation modeling.
output_examples.output: The response explains NDC to RxNorm or ATC mapping, hierarchy level selection, one-to-many handling, and documentation of unmapped codes.

output_examples.input: User asks how to make PyHealth results suitable for a research report.
output_examples.output: The response lists held-out evaluation, calibration checks, subgroup fairness analysis, confidence intervals, interpretability review, and clear clinical limitations.

best_practices: Use patient-level splits for clinical prediction unless a different split is explicitly justified.
Compare simple baselines with advanced models before claiming clinical machine learning gains.
Document dataset access, code mappings, label definitions, calibration, fairness checks, and clinical review boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not split visits randomly when patient overlap could leak information across train and test data.
Do not treat drug recommendation outputs as clinical orders without safety review and local validation.
Do not report model accuracy alone for imbalanced clinical outcomes.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants use PyHealth references for healthcare datasets, tasks, models, preprocessing, training, and evaluation.

faq.question: Does it include patient data?
faq.answer: No. It documents workflows and examples. Users must provide their own authorized clinical datasets.

faq.question: Can it diagnose patients?
faq.answer: No. It supports model development guidance and does not provide clinical diagnosis or treatment decisions.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What PyHealth areas are covered?
faq.answer: It covers datasets, medical coding, clinical tasks, models, preprocessing, training, evaluation, fairness, and interpretability.

faq.question: What should users verify before deployment?
faq.answer: Users should verify privacy compliance, data quality, leakage controls, calibration, subgroup performance, clinical review, and regulatory requirements.
